past_medical_history: Distal aortic arch aneurysm, status post total aortic arch replacement 6 weeks ago
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 7 years ago, well controlled on oral agents
Peripheral arterial disease with bilateral femoral artery calcification
Former smoker, 40 pack-year history, quit 5 years ago
family_history: Father died of aortic rupture at age 72
Mother had hypertension and type 2 diabetes
Brother with coronary artery disease, stented at age 60

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, 40 pack-year history, quit 5 years ago
social_history.exercise: Limited since surgery; previously walked 30 minutes daily

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without aphasia. Able to follow complex three-step commands. Attention intact on serial sevens.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. No facial asymmetry at rest. Right lower facial droop noted when asked to smile, sparing the forehead. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline. Hearing grossly intact.
neurological_exam.motor: Right upper extremity: 3/5 proximal, 2/5 grip strength, 3/5 wrist extension. Right lower extremity: 4/5 hip flexion, 4/5 knee extension, 3/5 ankle dorsiflexion. Left upper and lower extremities: 5/5 throughout. Mild right pronator drift present.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased pinprick and light touch sensation in right upper extremity (hand and forearm) and right lower extremity (foot and distal leg). Proprioception mildly impaired in right hand. Left side intact.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Left upper extremity 2+, left lower extremity 2+. Right upper extremity 3+, right lower extremity 3+ with upgoing plantar response (positive Babinski sign on the right). Left plantar response is flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Left finger-to-nose and heel-to-shin intact. Right finger-to-nose mildly dysmetric, limited by weakness. Right heel-to-shin impaired by weakness.
neurological_exam.gait: Unable to ambulate safely due to right lower extremity weakness. Stood briefly with assistance but demonstrated rightward listing.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 8. No hemineglect on double simultaneous stimulation. No meningismus. Left carotid bruit present on auscultation.

patient.chief_complaint: Recurrent episodes of right-sided arm and leg weakness over the past two weeks, with today's episode not resolving
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Kenji Takahashi is a 70-year-old right-handed man who presents to the emergency department with persistent right-sided hemiparesis that began approximately three hours ago. He has a significant recent surgical history, having undergone total aortic arch replacement for a distal aortic arch aneurysm approximately six weeks prior to this presentation. His postoperative course was initially uncomplicated, and he was discharged from the surgical service two weeks after the operation in stable condition. Beginning approximately two weeks ago, he began experiencing recurrent episodes of transient right-sided weakness involving his arm and leg, each lasting between 30 minutes and several hours before spontaneously resolving. He reports approximately five such episodes over the past two weeks, each following a similar pattern with sudden onset weakness and mild slurring of speech that would gradually improve. He was seen by his primary care physician after the second episode, and a follow-up was scheduled with his vascular surgeon, but no urgent workup was initiated at that time. Today at approximately 09:00, while eating breakfast, he experienced the sudden onset of right arm weakness causing him to drop his chopsticks, followed rapidly by difficulty with his right leg and mild difficulty speaking clearly. Unlike previous episodes, the symptoms have not improved over the past three hours. His wife drove him to the emergency department, arriving at 12:05. He denies any headache, visual disturbances, chest pain, or loss of consciousness. He reports no fever, recent infection, or medication changes. His postoperative imaging at the time of his surgery had noted a finding involving the left common carotid artery adjacent to the vascular graft, but he was told it was being managed conservatively.

findings.location: Left middle cerebral artery territory, predominantly involving the left precentral gyrus and adjacent frontal cortex (motor strip region)
findings.size: Approximately 2.4 x 1.8 cm in maximal axial dimensions

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true restricted diffusion
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Subtle hyperintensity suggesting onset beyond 3 hours
signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense signal in left frontal cortex

findings.mass_effect: No significant mass effect. No midline shift.
findings.borders: Margins follow cortical and subcortical distribution in left MCA superior division territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Irregular narrowing of the left internal carotid artery with reduced flow signal compared to the right. Intracranial left ICA signal attenuated. Left MCA flow signal present but diminished compared to right.
signal_characteristics.TOF: Reduced flow-related enhancement in the left ICA and proximal left MCA

additional_observations: Mild chronic small vessel ischemic changes bilaterally (Fazekas grade 1)
Right MCA, bilateral ACA, and bilateral PCA patent on MRA with normal caliber
No prior completed infarcts identified despite history of transient episodes
mri.impression: Acute left MCA superior division territory infarction with restricted diffusion involving the left precentral gyrus and adjacent frontal cortex. Significantly reduced flow in the left ICA and proximal left MCA on MRA, suspicious for upstream vascular pathology such as dissection or severe stenosis. No hemorrhagic transformation. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings: Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (SV1 + RV5 > 35 mm)
T-wave inversions in leads V5-V6, consistent with LVH strain pattern
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm at 76 bpm. Left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria with strain pattern. No arrhythmia detected. No acute ischemic changes.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: Hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL (L), Hematocrit 37.2 % (L), RBC 4.32 x10^12/L (L), BUN 24 mg/dL (H), Glucose 158 mg/dL (H), D-dimer 2.8 mg/L FEU (H), Total Cholesterol 212 mg/dL (H), LDL 128 mg/dL (H), Triglycerides 178 mg/dL (H), HbA1c 6.8 % (H), CRP 18.4 mg/L (H), ESR 42 mm/hr (H).

findings.location: Left common carotid artery extending into the left internal carotid artery
findings.size: Dissection extends approximately 8 cm from the junction with the aortic arch graft into the proximal left internal carotid artery

signal_characteristics.CTA: Intimal flap visible in the left common carotid artery starting approximately 7 cm distal to the aortic arch graft anastomosis. True lumen severely narrowed with false lumen opacified. Dissection extends cranially into the proximal left internal carotid artery. Significant reduction in true lumen caliber with estimated 80% stenosis at the most narrow segment.
signal_characteristics.Axial_reformats: Classic double-lumen sign with intimal flap separating true and false lumens. False lumen is larger than true lumen.

signal_characteristics.CTA: Delayed opacification of left MCA branches compared to right, consistent with reduced antegrade flow through the stenotic left ICA
Aortic arch graft in situ without endoleak. Anastomotic sites intact. Right common carotid and innominate artery patent.

additional_observations: Right common carotid and right internal carotid arteries patent with normal caliber
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with antegrade flow
No evidence of dissection in the vertebral arteries
Circle of Willis complete with patent anterior and posterior communicating arteries
output.impression: Left common carotid artery dissection with intimal flap entry point approximately 7 cm distal to the aortic arch graft, extending into the proximal left internal carotid artery with significant true lumen narrowing (estimated 80% stenosis). Reduced flow to the left hemisphere via the compromised left ICA. Aortic arch graft intact without endoleak. Clinical correlation recommended.

findings.location: Left common carotid artery and left internal carotid artery

signal_characteristics.B_mode: Intimal flap visualized in left CCA with double-lumen appearance. No significant calcified plaque.

findings.location: Right common carotid artery and right internal carotid artery

additional_observations: Left CCA demonstrates turbulent flow with spectral broadening distal to the graft anastomosis
Bilateral vertebral arteries patent with antegrade flow
output.impression: Hemodynamically significant stenosis of the left ICA (>70% by velocity criteria) secondary to carotid dissection with visible intimal flap on B-mode imaging. Normal right carotid system. Clinical correlation recommended.

Transthoracic Echocardiogram.value: Prosthetic aortic graft in situ, no paravalvular leak

output.interpretation: Abnormal values: Left Ventricular Wall Thickness Concentric LVH  (H), Pericardium Trivial pericardial effusion  (H).

findings: Normal sinus rhythm throughout the 24-hour recording
No episodes of atrial fibrillation or atrial flutter identified
Occasional premature atrial complexes (28 in 24 hours, <1% burden)
No ventricular tachycardia or significant ventricular ectopy
output.interpretation: Twenty-four hour Holter monitoring reveals normal sinus rhythm throughout with no atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other significant arrhythmia. Very low burden of premature atrial complexes.

output.query: carotid artery dissection post aortic surgery stroke management stenting

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection in Stroke Study (CADISS): Antiplatelet vs Anticoagulation
results.authors: Markus HS, Hayter E, et al.
results.key_finding: No significant difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for stroke prevention in cervical artery dissection. Both approaches are acceptable.

results.title: Stenting for Symptomatic Carotid Dissection: Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
results.key_finding: Carotid stenting for symptomatic dissection with recurrent events despite medical therapy shows high technical success rate (>95%) and low complication rate.

results.title: Management of Iatrogenic Carotid Artery Dissection After Cardiovascular Surgery
results.key_finding: Iatrogenic carotid dissection post cardiac/aortic surgery should be managed with anticoagulation initially; endovascular intervention reserved for recurrent ischemic events or progressive dissection.

output.summary: Carotid artery dissection can be managed with either antiplatelet or anticoagulation therapy per the CADISS trial. In cases of iatrogenic dissection post aortic surgery, anticoagulation is generally first-line. Endovascular stenting is indicated for recurrent ischemic events despite medical therapy, with high technical success rates. Given this patient's progression from TIAs to completed stroke despite aspirin therapy, endovascular intervention covering the entry point should be strongly considered.

interactions: Aspirin + Heparin: Increased bleeding risk. Consider holding aspirin during full-dose heparin therapy or close monitoring of aPTT and bleeding signs.
contraindications: Active intracranial hemorrhage (excluded by imaging in this patient)
Recent major surgery within 14 days — patient is 6 weeks post aortic arch replacement, which is beyond the high-risk window but warrants caution
warnings: Monitor aPTT every 6 hours and adjust to target 60-80 seconds
Monitor platelets every 2-3 days for heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
Mild anemia present — monitor hemoglobin closely for bleeding complications
CKD stage 2 — no dose adjustment required but monitor renal function
alternatives: Enoxaparin (LMWH) — avoid if creatinine clearance drops below 30 mL/min

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left MCA territory, secondary to left common carotid artery dissection extending into the internal carotid artery (iatrogenic, post aortic arch replacement)

differential.key_distinguishing: ECG and Holter monitoring show normal sinus rhythm throughout; echocardiogram shows no intracardiac thrombus or structural source
No witnessed seizure activity, transient episodes were stereotyped and lateralized without post-ictal confusion, and MRI confirms acute infarction
Blood glucose 158 mg/dL on presentation; HbA1c 6.8% indicates reasonably controlled diabetes without hypoglycemic episodes
Unilateral cortical signs (facial droop, UMN pattern), DWI-positive brain lesion; pattern inconsistent with spinal cord pathology

differential.diagnosis: Atherosclerotic large vessel disease with artery-to-artery embolism
differential.key_distinguishing: CTA demonstrates intimal flap and double-lumen sign characteristic of dissection rather than atherosclerotic plaque; onset temporally related to recent aortic surgery

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code; establish last known well time (3 hours) and perform NIHSS assessment
Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI or non-contrast CT head to assess for acute infarction and exclude hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left MCA territory infarction with restricted diffusion; reduced left ICA/MCA flow on MRA

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, D-dimer, and inflammatory markers
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated D-dimer, elevated CRP/ESR, normal coagulation, mild anemia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia as a cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm with LVH; no atrial fibrillation — argues against cardioembolic source

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CT angiography of head and neck to evaluate carotid artery pathology given surgical history and reduced ICA flow on MRA
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left CCA dissection with intimal flap extending into ICA with significant true lumen narrowing

optimal_actions.action: Consult vascular surgery and neurointerventional radiology for stent placement given recurrent ischemic events progressing to completed stroke despite antiplatelet therapy
Initiate systemic anticoagulation (IV heparin) to prevent further thromboembolic events from the dissection while planning definitive intervention
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Heparin safe to use; monitor for HIT and bleeding given recent surgery

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit or neurological ICU for close monitoring with serial neurological assessments
Search medical literature for management of carotid dissection post aortic surgery
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Endovascular stenting recommended for recurrent events despite medical therapy

optimal_actions.action: Order echocardiogram to exclude concurrent cardioembolic source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal cardiac function, no thrombus, ruling out cardiac embolism

critical_actions: Recognize that recurrent stereotyped transient hemiparesis in a patient with recent aortic arch surgery suggests carotid dissection rather than cardiac embolism
Obtain urgent brain imaging to confirm acute ischemic stroke and exclude hemorrhage
Obtain CT angiography to identify the left CCA dissection extending into the ICA as the stroke etiology
Exclude cardiac sources (ECG, echocardiogram) to confirm the vascular etiology
Initiate anticoagulation to prevent further thromboembolic events from the dissection
Consult vascular/neurointerventional surgery for definitive management (stenting) given failure of medical therapy
contraindicated_actions: Attributing the ischemic events to epilepsy without performing vascular imaging in a post-aortic surgery patient
Administering IV thrombolysis without first characterizing the carotid dissection, as stenting may be more appropriate
Delaying vascular imaging in a patient with recurrent ischemic events and recent aortic surgery
Performing lumbar puncture — not indicated and risks unnecessary delay
Aggressively lowering blood pressure below 140/90 in the acute phase, which could worsen hypoperfusion through the stenotic dissection
Discharging the patient on aspirin alone given progression of ischemic events despite ongoing antiplatelet therapy
key_reasoning_points: Recurrent stereotyped transient right-sided hemiparesis beginning weeks after aortic arch surgery should raise immediate suspicion for iatrogenic carotid artery dissection
The progression from TIAs to completed stroke despite aspirin therapy indicates inadequate medical management and need for escalation to anticoagulation and/or endovascular intervention
Normal sinus rhythm on ECG and normal echocardiogram exclude cardioembolic sources, redirecting attention to extracranial vascular pathology
MRA showing reduced left ICA and MCA flow provides the key clue pointing toward upstream carotid pathology rather than in-situ intracranial occlusion
CT angiography revealing the classic double-lumen sign with intimal flap originating near the aortic graft anastomosis confirms iatrogenic dissection as the etiology
Exclusion of alternative causes of transient paralysis (epilepsy, electrolyte abnormalities, hypoglycemia, spinal cord disease) is part of the systematic diagnostic process
Elevated D-dimer supports an active thrombotic process within the false lumen, consistent with ongoing dissection

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward — post-surgical patient with recurrent TIAs progressing to completed stroke, clear temporal relationship to aortic arch surgery, and classic carotid dissection findings on CTA
